description: The appliance status UI includes an Access navigation tab beside Pods and Logs.
Each pod row can preselect its first container and open the Access tab for a shell.
prdRefs: Users and Primary Flows > Open a container shell

description: Each pod row can preselect its first container and open the Access tab for a port forward.
prdRefs: Users and Primary Flows > Forward a pod port

description: The Access tab provides shared namespace, pod, and container selectors.
The pod summary API reports each container's declared TCP ports.
The Access tab renders an interactive terminal with @xterm/xterm.
The browser terminal fits its container and sends terminal dimension changes to Kubernetes.
The terminal toolbar offers Auto, bash, and sh shell choices.
Auto shell selection attempts bash and falls back to a new sh exec stream.
The terminal exposes explicit Connect and Disconnect controls with live connection state.
A browser tab maintains at most one active terminal and closes it when leaving Access.
The exec WebSocket requires a valid appliance management-session cookie.
Exec WebSockets and port-forward mutations reject cross-origin requests.
Exec and port-forward APIs validate Kubernetes names, shell choices, ports, durations, and request sizes.
The host service opens pod exec streams with the official Kubernetes JavaScript client.
The exec protocol relays terminal input, output, resize, ready, exit, and structured error messages.
Terminal input or output resets a 30-minute idle timeout.
Browser disconnect, logout, pod replacement, timeout, or control-plane shutdown closes the exec stream.
The appliance permits no more than four concurrent terminal sessions.
The host service disconnects slow terminal clients before WebSocket output buffering becomes unbounded.
The Access tab provides a form for creating a pod TCP port forward.
prdRefs: Users and Primary Flows > Forward a pod port

description: The port-forward form offers declared container ports and accepts a manual remote port.
An omitted local port receives an available port from the configured high-port range.
An administrator can request an explicit available appliance-side port.
A new forward can run for 30 minutes, 1 hour, 4 hours, or 8 hours.
The host service uses the Kubernetes JavaScript client to attach accepted TCP connections to the selected pod port.
The Access tab lists all active in-memory port forwards and their current state.
An active forward can be extended from the current time without exceeding eight hours.
prdRefs: Users and Primary Flows > Forward a pod port

description: Stopping a forward immediately closes its listener and active TCP connections.
prdRefs: Users and Primary Flows > Forward a pod port

description: The Access tab continuously warns that active forwarded ports are unauthenticated on the appliance LAN.
A forward binds only to the appliance IPv4 address that received its management request.
Pod deletion, UID replacement, or transition out of Running stops related forwards.
The appliance permits no more than sixteen active port forwards.
Control-plane shutdown closes every terminal, listener, and accepted TCP connection.
Terminal and forward lifecycle logs include targets and stop reasons but omit commands and payloads.
The UI displays structured validation, permission, shell, port, stream, timeout, and capacity failures.
The canonical control-plane ClusterRole grants create on pods/exec and pods/portforward.
Control-plane startup idempotently adds only the missing exec and port-forward rules to the known ClusterRole.
A failed RBAC migration leaves existing management features healthy and reports Access as unavailable.
The control-plane image packages the Kubernetes client, WebSocket server, xterm, and fit addon dependencies.
Existing setup, status, logs, recovery, update, and support-bundle routes continue to operate.
